skill.name: wp-block-themes
skill.description: Guides development and troubleshooting for WordPress block themes, including theme.json, templates, template parts, patterns, style variations, and Site Editor behavior.
skill.summary: Develop and debug WordPress block themes with focused guidance for theme.json, templates, patterns, and style variations.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious intent, no prompt injection, and no confirmed weak cryptography. Most command and crypto matches are Markdown backticks around WordPress terms, file paths, or documentation links. The only real concern is a local Node.js helper that reads the current repository to detect block theme folders.

low_findings.title: Local Repository Filesystem Enumeration
low_findings.description: The helper script walks the current working directory, reads theme.json files, and prints a report that includes the repository root. This is legitimate for theme detection, but it exposes local paths in command output and should only be run in intended project directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is directly implemented with fs.statSync, fs.readFileSync, and fs.readdirSync. Confidence is high that this is local enumeration, but context shows no network transmission or destructive writes.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are false positives. The flagged backticks surround WordPress file names, folders, and theme.json keys in Markdown, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows Markdown inline code spans and headings, not runtime code execution. The actual Node script contains no child_process, spawn, exec, eval, or shell invocation.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL False Positives
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded links to WordPress documentation and plugin pages. No file performs fetch, HTTP requests, package installation, or data upload.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain reference links in Markdown. There is no executable network client code in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography detections appear to match the text theme.json and URLs containing json. No hashing, encryption, password handling, or cryptographic API use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content discusses WordPress theme.json structure and debugging. Review found no crypto functions, weak algorithms, credential logic, or secret handling.

content.value_statement: WordPress block themes require precise theme.json, template, pattern, and style variation structure. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code targeted guidance for safe theme edits and debugging.
actual_capabilities: Identifies block theme roots by locating theme.json and template or part folders.
Guides safe edits to theme.json settings, presets, styles, and per-block rules.
Explains template, template part, pattern, and style variation folder conventions.
Helps debug Site Editor issues caused by user customizations or invalid theme.json shape.
Points to WordPress handbook references for current block theme behavior.
limitations: Does not create a full WordPress runtime or database for testing.
May require WP-CLI or a local WordPress site for complete verification.
Targets WordPress 6.9 and later guidance, so older projects need compatibility checks.
Does not replace visual review in the Site Editor and frontend.

use_cases.title: Create a New Block Theme
use_cases.description: Use the skill to plan the minimum file structure, choose a theme.json version, and verify block theme folders.

use_cases.title: Debug Style Overrides
use_cases.description: Use the skill to check whether user customizations, child themes, or invalid theme.json data override expected styles.

use_cases.title: Maintain Theme Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the skill to organize filesystem patterns, templates, parts, and style variations without breaking editor expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Find My Block Theme
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-block-themes skill to inspect this repository and identify the active block theme structure.

prompt_templates.title: Add Theme Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-block-themes skill to add editor-visible color and spacing presets to my theme.json.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Site Editor Styles
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-block-themes skill to explain why my Site Editor styles differ from the frontend and propose checks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Theme Conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-block-themes skill to plan a classic theme to block theme conversion with templates, parts, patterns, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Inspect my theme folders and tell me whether this is a block theme.
output_examples.output: A concise report naming the theme root, whether theme.json exists, and which templates, parts, patterns, and styles folders are present.

output_examples.input: My theme.json color changes do not appear in the Site Editor.
output_examples.output: A debugging checklist covering active theme selection, user customizations, style hierarchy, and theme.json structure validation.

output_examples.input: Add a new style variation for my theme.
output_examples.output: A file placement plan, naming guidance, compatibility notes, and verification steps for Site Editor and frontend behavior.

best_practices: Confirm the target theme root before editing any theme files.
Validate theme.json structure after changing settings, styles, or block rules.
Test changes in both the Site Editor and frontend because overrides can differ.
anti_patterns: Editing an inactive theme and assuming the Site Editor will reflect the change.
Using custom CSS before checking whether theme.json supports the required style.
Changing a selected style variation without testing reset or fresh-user behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill edit WordPress files directly?
faq.answer: It can guide edits and run a local detection script. Actual changes depend on the user request and repository state.

faq.question: Does it require a running WordPress site?
faq.answer: Repository inspection does not require WordPress. Visual and Site Editor verification usually needs a local or remote WordPress site.

faq.question: Can it work with classic themes?
faq.answer: It can help plan conversion, but its guidance focuses on block theme structure and theme.json behavior.

faq.question: Why do theme.json changes sometimes not appear?
faq.answer: User customizations, child themes, invalid structure, or editing the wrong theme can override or hide theme defaults.

faq.question: Does the skill contact external services?
faq.answer: No executable network behavior was found. The files include documentation links to WordPress resources.

faq.question: Is the included script safe to run?
faq.answer: It reads the current repository and prints a summary. Run it only inside project directories you intend to inspect.
